coredata.dc:title: pH-responsive capsule loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for metal corrosion protection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Chengbin
Lian, Yubo
Huang, Chen
Chen, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a stimuli-release system based on mesoporous silica (MSN) loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the core material and alginate cheating with Ca2+ as the wall materials were developed. The N-CDs were first synthesized via the solvothermal method, and then MSN@N-CDs were fabricated by stirring for 24 h. The controlled release system (MSN@N-CDs@Alg) was based on alginate cheating with Ca2+. The microstructure and chemical composition of N-CDs, MSN@N-CDs, and MSN@N-CDs@Alg were investigated. The pH-responsive release behavior and anticorrosion performance of the designed system were discussed. Results showed that the prepared N-CDs had a size distribution of 3–5 nm, and the loaded efficiency of MSN@N-CDs was 7.56%. Ultraviolet spectrophotometry measurements confirmed that the prepared capsule exhibited a higher release rate in alkaline conditions. Electrochemical measurements demonstrated that the capsule can release N-CDs intelligently following the corrosion degree of metal to improve the corrosion resistance. This safe, low-toxicity, and simple-to-prepare method can provide a new strategy for metal protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica
Carbon dots
pH-responsive release
Corrosion inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of N-CDs loaded into MSN
Surface analysis and contact angle measurement
Release behavior of the prepared capsule
Removal route of CTAB in the preparation process of MSN
Mechanism of pH-responsive release for metal protection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed synthesis mechanism of N-CDs and the capsule.

The microstructure and compositions of N-CDs (a) TEM image (b) HRTEM image (c) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of N-CDs (Inset: photographs of N-CDs dispersed in ethanol under natural light and UV light at 365 nm) (d) FT-IR spectra of p-PD and N-CDs (e) Raman spectra of p-PD and N-CDs (f) Full XPS spectra of p-PD and N-CDs.
TEM images of the prepared MSN (a) at low magnification (b) at high magnification (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm and (d) pore size distribution of the prepared MSN.

Characterizations of MSN@N-CDs (a) HRTEM image (b) EDS mapping image of N element (c) EDS mapping image of Si element (d) composite EDS mapping image of N and Si elements (e) the TGA curve of MSN@N-CDs.
Characterizations of the prepared MSN@N-CDs@Alg capsule (a) optical image (b–c) SEM images at different magnification (d) EDS mapping images of C, O, Ca, Si elements, respectively.
(a) The release behavior of the prepared capsule at different pH solutions and (b) the fitted curve with the Ritger-Peppas model.
Nyquist and Bode plots for Q235 carbon steel immersed in corrosive medium (a–b) pH = 3 (c–d) pH = 6 (e–f) pH = 9.
Polarization curves of carbon steel measured at (a) pH = 3 (b) pH = 6 (c) pH = 9.
The contact angle of carbon steel measured at different pH cases.

The macroscopic and microscopic corrosion morphology for carbon steel measured at blank condition, presence of the prepared capsule, presence of N-CDs, respectively (from top to bottom) in the cases of (a–c) pH = 3 (d–f) pH = 6 (g–i) pH = 9.
The TEM images of MSN (a) prepared by calcination method (b) prepared by reflux method.
Mechanism of (a) capsule formation and (b) drug release.
The corrosion protection mechanism of the prepared capsule for Q235 carbon steel.
The element content of C, O and N atoms for p-PD and N-CDs.
Fitting parameter for N-CDs release calculated by non-linear regression under different pH conditions.
Fitting electrochemical parameters obtained from EIS measurements in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solutions with different pH values.
Polarization parameters of carbon steel in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solutions with different pH values.

head.ce:title: pH-responsive capsule loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for metal corrosion protection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Material Chemistry of Energy Conversion and Storage, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology Wuhan, 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Material Chemistry of Energy Conversion and Storage
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology Wuhan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Material Chemistry of Energy Conversion and Storage, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology Wuhan 430074, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xi'an Changqing Chemical Group Co. Ltd, Xi'an, 710018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xi'an Changqing Chemical Group Co. Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xi’an Changqing Chemical Group Co. Ltd, Xi’an 710018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a stimuli-release system based on mesoporous silica (MSN) loaded with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the core material and alginate cheating with Ca2+ as the wall materials were developed. The N-CDs were first synthesized via the solvothermal method, and then MSN@N-CDs were fabricated by stirring for 24 h. The controlled release system (MSN@N-CDs@Alg) was based on alginate cheating with Ca2+. The microstructure and chemical composition of N-CDs, MSN@N-CDs, and MSN@N-CDs@Alg were investigated. The pH-responsive release behavior and anticorrosion performance of the designed system were discussed. Results showed that the prepared N-CDs had a size distribution of 3–5 nm, and the loaded efficiency of MSN@N-CDs was 7.56%. Ultraviolet spectrophotometry measurements confirmed that the prepared capsule exhibited a higher release rate in alkaline conditions. Electrochemical measurements demonstrated that the capsule can release N-CDs intelligently following the corrosion degree of metal to improve the corrosion resistance. This safe, low-toxicity, and simple-to-prepare method can provide a new strategy for metal protection.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of the economy, metal has played a crucial part in infrastructure and energy extraction [1,2]. However, how to reduce the economic losses, safety risks, and environmental pollution caused by metal corrosion in practical applications has become an urgent problem for scientific researchers to solve [3]. In the past decades, various methods, such as cathodic protection [4,5], anodic passivation [6,7], surface treatment [8,9], and adding corrosion inhibitors [10,11], have been proposed to improve the corrosion resistance of metals. Among these strategies, adding corrosion inhibitors has become the most efficient method [12]. These molecules with polar groups can easily adsorb on the metal surface to form a thin film and prevent the corrosion of the metal. However, directly adding excessive corrosion inhibitor molecules can easily cause environmental pollution. A large number of corrosion inhibitor molecules will also be degraded in the corrosive medium, which cannot provide long-term protection for metals [13]. These problems greatly limit the application of corrosion inhibitors.

In recent years, the development of controlled-release systems can address the above problems well [14,15]. Materials, such as SiO2, halloysite nanotubes (HNT), carbon nanotubes, and clay minerals were considered the ideal candidates for corrosion inhibitor encapsulation [16–18]. These nanocontainers, which encapsulated corrosion inhibitor molecules, were utilized to design the stimuli-release systems [19]. To date, researchers have extended these external stimuli to many fields, such as pH [20], heat [21], electric field [22], magnetic field [23], and ion strength [24]. Among these stimuli, research on the pH stimuli-release system should be the most important because during the metal corrosion process, the corrosion behavior of the “large cathodic, small anode,” easily causes changes in the pH of different metal corrosive position points. Once the pH-controlled-release system perceives the change in pH values, the corrosion inhibitor molecules can be released to protect metals. Jacob Ress et al. [25] prepared a pH-controlled microcapsule containing NaNO2 via a water-in-oil-in-water double emulsion method. The microcapsules exhibit an obvious acid sensitivity, and the encapsulated NaNO2 molecules can efficiently improve the corrosion resistance of carbon steel. Yang et al. [26] fabricated BTA@ZIF-8 nanoparticles via a one-pot method. The prepared BTA@ZIF-8 nanoparticles possess a nature of acid sensitivity and provide a self-healing ability for composite coating.

Mesoporous silica (MSN) exhibits good biocompatibility, uniform pore distribution, and large specific surface area, which can use to load inhibitor molecules [27]. Sodium alginate (SA), a biodegradable material with efficient carboxyl groups, can interact with divalent cations, such as Ca2+, and then induce a gelation reaction under mild conditions [28]. At different pH, the 3D network structure formed by alginate has different densities, which indicates that alginate has a good pH sensitivity and can employ to design a controlled-release system. Therefore, the combination of SiO2 and alginate will realize the intelligent release of corrosion inhibitors.

Traditional corrosion inhibitors molecules, such as benzotriazole, 2-mercaptobenzothiazole, and quinoline, are mostly toxic organic, which cause significant harm to the environment and the human body [29]. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CDs) with high efficiency and low toxicity have emerged [30]. CDs possess many unique properties, including facile preparation, excellent water solubility, tunable emission, high photostability, and excellent biocompatibility. Based on above characteristics, CDs have been widely used in biomedical fields like chem/biosensing, bioimaging, and nanomedicine [31–33]. In addition, CDs also exhibit great application potential in metal corrosion protection. Compared with traditional corrosion inhibitors, CDs have the advantages of a simple synthesis method and low biological toxicity [34]. Cui et al. [35] reported that N-doped CDs in 1 M hydrochloric acid greatly improve the corrosion resistance of carbon steel. Subsequently, Cen et al. [36]. further increased the doping elements of CDs and fabricated N, S-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) with salicylic acid and thiourea as raw materials. The results show that these N, S-CDs can remarkably improve the anti-corrosion performance. Such environmentally friendly CDs can delay the corrosion of metals extremely. However, the combination of CDs and a stimuli-release system has yet to be reported.

Based on the above literature research, we proposed a stimulated release system with N-CDs as the corrosion inhibitor. MSN was employed as the nanocontainer to encapsulate N-CDs, and the 3D network structure formed by alginate and Ca2+ worked as the gatekeeper to control the release behavior of N-CDs. The scheme of the preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The pH stimuli-release behavior of the prepared capsule was investigated, and electrochemical measurements explored the metal protection. The results showed that this intelligent controlled-release system could protect metals depending on the pH of the environment.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99%) and cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD. p-phenylenediamine (p-PD), Sodium alginate (SA, Mw = 398.31), sodium chloride (NaCl), anhydrous calcium chloride (CaCl2), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were all with 99% purity and were furnished from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

The Q235 carbon steel was supplied by the Shandong metal material processing plant with 94.5% purity (composition: 0.001 wt.% B, 0.002 wt.% Sb, 0.002 wt.% As, 0.005 wt.% Ni, 0.003 wt.% Pb, 0.005 wt.% Zn, balanced with iron).

MSN was synthesized by using the method proposed by Su et al. [37]. In detail, 1 g CTAB was first dissolved in 500 mL deionized water, and then 280 mg NaOH was introduced to the above solution. The mixture's temperature was raised to 80 °C under vigorous stirring for 20 min. Subsequently, 5 mL TEOS was instilled into the surfactant solution at a 0.5 mL/min speed. The mixture was stirred for another 2 h after the above processes were completed. The white precipitate formed was washed with deionized water and dried under vacuum at 40 °C overnight.

CTAB was removed by reflux etching. Concentrated hydrochloric acid (5 mL) and ethanol (100 mL) were thoroughly mixed in a flask, and then 1 g of the fabricated silica was added into the mixture and refluxed for 15 h. The MSN was collected and rinsed with ethanol several times. The solid was dried at 60 °C for 6 h.

The preparation procedure of the N-CDs followed the research of Bao et al. [38]. Typically, 0.6 g p-PD was added into 60 mL ethanol and ultrasound for 5 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-lined reactor and kept at 200 °C for 18 h. The crude product was then dialyzed through a dialysis membrane for one day, and the purified N-CDs were obtained after the hydrothermal reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of N-CDs loaded into MSN
ce:section.ce:para: MSN (1 g) and N-CDs (2 g) were introduced into 150 mL ethanol solution and stirred for 8 h. The suspension was transferred to a vacuum drying oven and pumped to a vacuum of −0.1 MPa. Bubbles in the suspension indicated that the gas in MSN was removed. The suspension returned to atmospheric pressure after 1 h in a vacuum. The above procedure was repeated three times to improve the encapsulation efficiency. The mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm to remove the supernatant. The obtained solid was washed with ethanol three times and dried at 60 °C overnight. The received product was named as MSN@N-CDs.

The scheme of the preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1(b). First, 0.5 g SA was added to 20 mL deionized water and then stirred for 30 min to completely dissolve the SA.MSN@N-CDs (0.2 g) were introduced into the above solution and stirred for 10 min after the dissolution process was completed. Subsequently, the mixture was dripped into a 2% calcium chloride solution via a disposable syringe under magnetic stirring (200 rpm) for 5 min. Finally, the MSN@N-CDs coated with a 3D network structure were formed and defined as MSN@N-CDs@Alg.

The morphology and microstructure of MSN, N-CDs, and MSN@N-CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The surface area and pore size distribution of the prepared MSN were confirmed via the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) methods. The morphology of MSN@N-CDs@Alg was investigated by using a digital camera and a scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The chemical composition, crystal structure, and element valence of the prepared samples were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50R, America), X-ray diffraction (x'pert3 powder, Netherlands), Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600W, Japan). The loading efficiency of N-CDs was confirmed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) with a 10 °C/min heating rate from 20 °C to 800 °C under an N2 atmosphere.

MSN@N-CDs@Alg (300 mg) was immersed into 200 mL of 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution with pH values at 3, 6, and 9. The supernatant (2 mL) was aspirated, and the fresh solution (2 mL) was replaced after a specific time interval. Subsequently, the supernatant was investigated via an ultraviolet (UV) spectrophotometer at 430 nm. The percentage of drug release was calculated by using the following equation
(1)
p e r c e n t a g e
o f
d r u g
r e l e a s e =
M t
M ∞
× 100 % ,
where
M t
is the release amount at time t, and
M ∞
represents the maximum drug release amount.

The electrochemical tests were utilized to evaluate the corrosion protection of the prepared capsules for Q235 carbon steel. In the electrochemical experiments, a three-electrode system was adopted. In detail, Q235 carbon steel acted as a working electrode, the saturated calomel electrode worked as a reference electrode, and the Pt electrode was employed to serve as the counter electrode. As preparation for testing, the carbon steel electrode was encapsulated with epoxy resin with an area of 1 cm2. The working surface was polished with sandpaper in increasing order (400, 1200, 2000 grits) and rinsed with ethanol several times.

The anti-corrosion performance of the released N-CDs on carbon steel was investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization. The carbon steel working electrode was immersed in a test solution for 0.5 h to obtain a steady open circuit potential before the electrochemical measurements. EIS was measured at open circuit potential, with alternating current signals of 5 mV amplitude and scanning frequency ranging from 100 kHz to 10 mHz. For the potentiodynamic polarization measurements, the scanning range corresponded from −150 mV to +150 mV relative to the OCP along with the 0.5 mV/s scanning rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface analysis and contact angle measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The digital camera (Canon EOS R5) and SEM (Hitachi SU8010) were utilized to investigate the corrosion morphology of the metal surface, and contact angle measurement (JC 2000) was employed to evaluate the wettability of the specimen surface. This process was performed to understand better the protective ability of the prepared capsule for Q235 carbon steel. The samples were immersed in an aggressive medium for 5 h before the SEM and contact angle tests. Subsequently, the specimens were dried through a hairdryer.

The microstructure of the fabricated N-CDs was observed by TEM. As depicted in Fig. 2 (a), the prepared N-CDs were uniformly dispersed and had a narrow size distribution with an average diameter of about 3 nm. The HRTEM (Fig. 2(b)) image exhibited the prominent lattice structure with a 0.236 nm spacing. Fig. 2(c) exhibits the optical properties of p-PD and N-CDs. The raw material of p-PD exhibited two absorption peaks at 243 and 294 nm on the UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra. The peak located at 243 nm corresponded to the Π–Π∗ transition of CC [39]. The appearance of the peak at 294 nm was caused by n–Π∗ transition of the CO bond or other connected groups [40]. A new absorption peak was observed at 430 nm after the solvothermal reaction, consistent with the exciton transition in the quinoid-imine units of the long-conjugated polymer chain [41]. The photoluminescence property of N-CDs was measured under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm, and the spectrum exhibited a peak at 596 nm. The UV–vis spectrum of results of p-PD and N-CDs indicated the occurrence of energy transition during the preparation process. The results were consistent with the reports of Cui et al. [35].

The FT-IR, Raman, and XPS spectra were utilized to characterize the p-PD and N-CDs. This process was performed to better distinguish the chemical functional groups and the variations of the chemical bonds. Fig. 2(d) depicts the FTIR spectra of p-PD and N-CDs. Compared with p-PD, three new infrared peaks appeared at 2860, 1556, and 1230 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of N-CDs. The new peaks at 2860, 1556, and 1230 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of C–H, C–N = , and C–O, respectively [42]. The appearance of new infrared characteristic peaks confirmed the chemical reaction between p-PD molecules.

The Raman spectra of p-PD and N-CDs are depicted in Fig. 2(e) p-PD exhibited five characteristic peaks at 647, 843, 1174, 1264, and 1612 cm−1, respectively, which were assigned to the breathing vibration of the benzene ring, deformation vibration of the benzene ring, C–H bending in-plane, stretching of C–N, and the stretching of the benzene ring, respectively. After the solvothermal reaction, the N-CDs exhibit two featured bands. The peak located at 1397 cm−1 is assigned to the C–N+ stretching of the delocalized polaronic charge carriers. The band at 1522 cm−1 corresponds to the N–H bending deformation mode and the CC deformation of benzenoid rings and quinoid rings.

The full XPS spectra of p-PD and N-CDs are displayed in Fig. 2(f), and the content of the corresponding elements is listed in Table 1 . In the full XPS spectra, the three peaks at 285, 540, and 650 eV, corresponding to C, N, and O were detected for p-PD and N-CDs. The content of N and O elements detected between p-PD and N-CDs differed remarkably. In accordance with the results, the N content of N-CDs increased to 18.21% compared with the 2.16% content of p-PD. The O content decreased to 5.61% compared with the 18.96% content. The variations of the O element content indicated the occurrence of intermolecular cyclization and condensation reaction during the solvothermal reaction, and the increase in the N element content implied the successful doping of N into the carbon dots. The high-resolution spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s is depicted in Figs. S1(a–c). For C 1s, two peaks located at 285.2 and 284.2 eV were observed, which were related to CN and CC, respectively [43]. The O 1s were fitted with a single peak at 532 eV, which was attributed to C–OH/C–O–C [38]. The N1s were analyzed into two peaks, corresponding to pyridinic N (398.8 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.4 eV), respectively [44]. The fitted results of XPS spectra confirmed the successful preparation of N-CDs.

The micro-silica nanoparticles were employed to act as nanocarriers for encapsulating the N-CDs. The microstructure and morphology were confirmed by TEM and BET tests. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a), the TEM image confirmed that the prepared MSN exhibited a spherical structure, and the pore canal was visually observed. BET measurements further determined the pore size distribution. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm exhibited a typical type IV curve with an obvious hysteresis loop in Fig. 3(c), indicating that the prepared MSN was attributed to mesoporous material. The pore size distribution was measured by using the BJH method. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the pore size distribution of MSN was narrow and centered at 4.50 nm. The BET surface area was 716.3548 m2g−1, and the pore volume was 1.2877 cm3g−1. The results of TEM and BET tests demonstrated that the prepared MSN could be an ideal candidate for corrosion inhibitor encapsulation.

TEM measurements were implemented to visually confirm whether N-CDs were encapsulated. As depicted in Fig. 4 (a), the microstructure of MSN did not change after the loading process of N-CDs, indicating that the loading process cannot change the morphology of MSN. The EDS mapping of MSN@N-CDs is depicted in Fig. 4(b–d). The element signal of N was detected. To the best of our knowledge, the N element was caused by the N-doped carbon dots. Therefore, the TEM image and EDS mapping results implied the successful loading of N-CDs into MSN.

The TGA measurements were employed to further estimate the loading efficiency of N-CDs. Fig. 4(e) illustrates the TGA curves of N-CDs, MSN, and MSN@N-CDs. No remarkable mass loss of MSN was observed during the whole measurement, which indicated that the prepared MSN obtained excellent thermal stability, and the surfactant of CTAB used in the preparation process was etched completely. In the case of N-CDs, it was found a remarkable mass loss stage from 100 °C to 250 °C, which was related to the thermal decomposition of N-CDs. MSN@N-CDs exhibited two distinct stages of mass loss. The first stage beginning from room temperature to 100 °C was caused by the evaporation of water. With the increase in temperature, the mass loss appeared from 110 °C to 500 °C, and the percentage of mass loss was about 7.56%. Compared with the mass loss stage of N-CDs, the mass loss process of MSN@N-CDs continued up to 500 °C, indicating that the N-CDs molecules loaded in the pore channel of MSN require a higher temperature for decomposition. In summary, the results of TGA measurements confirmed the loading of N-CDs molecules into the MSN, and the loading efficiency was 7.56%.

The holistic appearance of the MSN@N-CDs@Alg was characterized via an electronic digital camera, as depicted in Fig. 5 (a). The prepared capsule exhibited a spherical morphology, and the diameter was about 3 mm. The morphology and structure of MSN@N-CDs@Alg were characterized via SEM at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. The MSN@N-CD@Alg was frozen with a freeze dryer for 24 h before the SEM observation. The capsule was cut open with a scalpel, exposing the entire inner layer for SEM tests. Fig. 5(b and c) depict the SEM images of MSN@N-CD@Alg. The cross-section showed undulating folds due to the chelation of SA with calcium ions. Some dark attachments were observed on the cross-section, which can be considered the MSN@N-CDs. Under the higher magnification (Fig. 5(c)), the film formed by SA and calcium ions obtained an orderly fold. Some pores were found on the surface of the capsule, which can be bubbles introduced during the preparation process. EDS mapping was utilized to distinguish the elements and further determine the dark attachments' chemical composition. As illustrated in Fig. 5(e), the element signals of C, O, Ca, and Si were detected in the detected area. The elements of C, O, and Ca confirmed the successful chelation of SA with calcium ions. The appearance of the Si element indicated the loading of MSN into Alg. However, the element of N, which belongs to N-CDs, was not found. The N-CDs loaded in the pore channel of MSN; thus, the EDS mapping failed to detect the N element.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release behavior of the prepared capsule
ce:section.ce:para: The pH stimuli-release behavior of the prepared capsule was investigated via a UV spectrophotometer, and the scanning wavelength ranged from 200 nm to 800 nm. The accumulative release percentage of N-CDs in different pH conditions is depicted in Fig. 6 (a). In the case of pH = 3, the slowest release behavior was detected, and the released amount of N-CDs reached 10% in 5 h. With the increase in pH value to 6, the released amount of N-CDs came to 70% during immersion. When the pH was adjusted to 9, the released amount achieved a maximum value of 90%, indicating the release of approximately all the N-CDs molecules during immersion. In an alkaline medium, N-CDs had the most remarkable release amount and the fastest release rate. With the decrease in pH value, the release rate and release amount are gradually suppressed. The investigation of the release behavior affirmed that the prepared capsule obtained a pH stimuli-release property and may exhibit a bright application prospect in metal protection.

The Ritger–Peppas model was utilized to fit the data and to better understand the controlled-release mechanism of the prepared capsule [45].
(2)
M t
M ∞
= k
t n
,
where
M t
/
M ∞
is the release ratio at time t, k is the release rate constant, and n is the release exponent. The fitted lines are depicted in Fig. 6(b), and the above-fitted data are listed in Table 2 .

In accordance with the results of Fig. 6(b) and Table 2, the release rate constant k increased from 0.002 to 0.07 and reached 0.089, indicating that the release rate of the drug molecules increased with the improvement of pH values. By the previous reports, n > 0.85 implied that the drug release behavior was controlled by case II transport. When n < 0.85, the drug release behavior can be considered as Fickian diffusion or anomalous diffusion to hold [46]. For the prepared MSN@N-CDs@Alg capsule, the values of n were 0.74, 0.37, and 0.46 in the cases of pH = 3, pH = 6, and pH = 9, respectively. The fitted results indicated that the controlled-release mechanism of the prepared capsule was the cooperation of drug diffusion and swelling of beads.

In the acid environment, the release rate constant k was 0.002, which was much lower than that of pH = 6 and pH = 9. Considering the controlled-release mechanism, the lower k value can be explained that in the acid environment, the 3D network of SA–Ca was shrunk and restricted the release of N-CDs. The Ritger–Peppas model better explained that the release rate of N-CDs was controlled by the diffusion of drug molecules and the swelling of beads [28].

Fick's second law was utilized to model the diffusion process of N-CDs and to further determine the trend of diffusion coefficients in different pH systems during the diffusion of drug molecules [47]. The model equations can be defined as
(3)
∂ C
∂ t
= D
[
∂ 2
C
∂ r
2
+
2 r
∂ C
∂ r
]
.

With the boundary conditions: t = 0 0<r<a C
C 1

t > 0 r = a C
C 0,

where the release behavior of the prepared capsule was considered a 1D radial release from a sphere of radius r. D is the diffusion coefficient of N-CDs. t represents the release time, and a denotes the radius of the prepared capsule. C 1 is the initial concentration of N-CDs within the prepared capsule, and C 0 is the initial concentration of N-CDs in the corrosive medium.

By the research of Berens and Huvard et al., the above model equations can be rewritten as [47–49].
(4)
M t
M ∞
= 1 −
∑
n = 0
∞
8
(
2 n + 1
)
2
π 2
exp ⁡ [
− D
(
2 n + 1
)
2
π 2
t
4
a 2
] .

In this way, we calculated the diffusion coefficient (D) for N-CDs released from the medium of pH = 3, pH = 6, and pH = 9. The obtained values were 5.52 × 10−13, 1.49 × 10−11, and 2.65 × 10−11 m2/s. A positive correlation was observed between the D value and the pH value, and the results were consistent with the fitted results of release kinetics.

In summary, the release behavior of the prepared capsule was controlled by drug diffusion and swelling of beads. The diffusion coefficient (D) was suppressed in the acid medium, indicating that the designed capsule obtained a pH stimuli-release property. The pH-controlled release property of the prepared capsule can be explained that, under acidic conditions, most carboxylic acid groups can combine with hydrogen ions to form carboxylic groups (--COOH), which weaken the electrostatic repulsion between anionic groups, thereby inhibiting the swelling of MSN@N-CDs@Alg and impede the release of N-CDs. However, more carboxylic acid groups were ionized when the capsules were immersed in alkaline environments, which enhanced repulsion and promoted the expansion of the polymer matrix and the release of N-CDs.

EIS measurements were adopted to further confirm the corrosion protection efficiency of the prepared capsule. Fig. 7 exhibits the EIS curves received from carbon steel immersed in (a) 3.5 wt.% NaCl, pH = 3 (b) 3.5 wt.% NaCl, pH = 6, and (c) 3.5 wt.% NaCl, pH = 9, with and without the prepared corrosion inhibitor capsule for 3 h. A control test that directly added N-CDs to the corrosive medium was conducted to compare the inhibition efficiency of the prepared capsule.

In the control experiment, the added concentration of N-CDs was determined in accordance with the results of TGA measurements. For example, 200 ppm MSN@N-CDs were selected to prepare the capsule, and the loading efficiency measured by TGA measurement was 7.56%. Therefore, the concentration of N-CDs directly added should be 15.12 ppm.

Fig. 7(a–f) illustrate the Nyquist and Bode plots obtained from 3.5 wt.% NaCl with pH 3, 6, 9. In the case of pH = 3, the impedance spectroscopy showed the largest diameter in the controlled system, indicating that the N-CDs can provide an excellent protection effect for metal. The impedance modulus values of the prepared capsule were much smaller, and an inductance appeared, implying that the metal was seriously corroded. However, the impedance modulus values were slightly enlarged compared with the blank condition with the addition of the prepared capsule, indicating that the prepared capsule can protect the Q235 carbon steel under the condition of pH = 3. However, the effect was unremarkable, which may be due to the insufficient release of N-CDs. In the case of pH = 6, the EIS curves showed a similar tendency to the case of pH = 3. With the addition of N-CDs, the capacitive arc obtained the largest diameter, indicating the best protection effect for Q235 carbon steel. For the prepared capsule, the impedance modulus expanded remarkably with the increase in pH value, which proved that the release of N-CDS in the capsule was further increased. With the increase in pH value to 9, the values of impedance modulus obtained in the prepared capsule system remarkably enlarged, and the values of impedance modulus were much closer to that of N-CDs. This finding suggested that in high pH conditions, the inhibition effect of the prepared capsule is the same as that of directly adding N-CDs.
The results of EIS measurements demonstrated that the prepared capsule could play a more positive role in the anticorrosion performance with the increase in pH values.

As shown in Fig. S2, the equivalent circuit diagram of impedance spectra(a) was utilized to fit the EIS data obtained from the blank system and designed system in the case of pH = 3. The equivalent circuit diagram of impedance spectra(b) was utilized to fit the other EIS curves. Rs is the resistance of the solution between the reference electrode and the metal/working electrode. Rct denotes the charge transfer resistance of carbon steel and can be utilized to assess the corrosion level. Rf represents the film resistance on the surface of the working electrode. CPEdl is the constant phase element related to the double-layer capacitance, and CPEf represents the capacitance of the film. L is the inductive reactance. Rp is the sum of Rf and Rct. The related electrochemical parameters obtained from EIS measurements are listed in Table 3 . The corrosion inhibition efficiency was calculated in accordance with the following equation [50].
(5)
η
( % )
=
R p
−
R p 0
R p
× 100 % ,
where Rp is the sum of Rf and Rct in the presence of the prepared capsule or N-CDs. Rp’ is the sum of Rf and Rct in the blank system.

As shown in Table 3, the CPEct values for the prepared capsule and N-CDs decreased compared with that of the blank condition in all systems with different pH values. This finding can be explained that the water molecules with larger permittivity were gradually replaced by N-CDs, which reduced the double capacitance constant of the metal surface [51]. For the prepared capsule, the values of Rp gradually increased with the improvement of pH values. The corrosion efficiency increased from 15% to 49.36% when the pH value increased from 3 to 6. In the case of pH = 9, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the prepared capsule reached 95%, indicating the excellent protection effect for Q235 carbon steel. The fitting results of EIS measurements further demonstrated that the corrosion inhibition effect of the prepared capsules floated with the pH values and provided an excellent protection effect at high pH values.

Fig. 8 exhibits the potentiodynamic polarization curves received from carbon steel immersed in (a) pH = 3, 3.5 wt.% NaCl, (b) pH = 6, 3.5 wt.% NaCl, and (c) pH = 9, 3.5 wt.% NaCl with and without the prepared corrosion inhibitor capsule for 3 h. A control test that directly added N-CDs to the corrosive medium was conducted to compare the inhibition efficiency of the prepared capsule. In the case of pH = 3 and pH = 6, the polarization was fitted in the weak polarized region. In the case of pH = 9, the polarization was fitted in the Tafel region. The fitted results are listed in Table 4 .

In the case of pH = 3, the polarization curve overlapped with the blank condition and exhibited a high value of corrosion current density (I corr) with the addition of the prepared capsule, indicating that the corrosion inhibitor capsule had a slight protective effect on carbon steel under acidic conditions. With the addition of N-CDs, the corrosion potential (E corr) shifted positively to approximately 25 mV, and the I corr value shifted negatively to 8.7871 × 10−6 A/cm2, demonstrating that the prepared N-CDs can improve the anticorrosion performance of carbon steel.

In the case of pH = 6, the anticorrosion performance of the carbon steel immersed in the capsule corrosive medium remarkably improved. The I corr value shifted negatively to 8.1062 × 10−6 A/cm2, which was much lower than that of the blank condition (1.2709 × 10−5 A/cm2). With the introduction of N-CDs, the value of I corr further decreased to 4.1162 × 10−6 A/cm2, indicating that the prepared capsules did not achieve the best protective effect in the case of pH = 6, which may be related to the insufficient release of N-CDs. When the pH value increased to 9, the most excellent anticorrosion performance was investigated. As shown in Fig. 8(c), Ecorr shifted positively, and I corr shifted negatively with the addition of the prepared capsule or N-CDs. For the prepared capsule, the value of I corr was found to be 2.6195 × 10−6 A/cm2 and much closer to that of the addition of N-CDs (2.2683 × 10−6 A/cm2). The corrosion inhibition efficiency was calculated, as listed in Table 4.

The corrosion inhibition efficiency of the prepared capsule immersed in pH = 3, 6, and 9 was 15%, 50%, and 85%, respectively. The increase in the corrosion inhibition efficiency demonstrated that the released amount of N-CDs gradually increased with the increase in pH values. The results of potentiodynamic polarization were consistent with the results of the release behavior and EIS measurements.

Contact angle measurements were conducted to identify the adsorption amount of the N-CD molecules on the specimen surface. Fig. 9 displays the contact angle of Q235 carbon steel after immersion in the aggressive solution for 3 h.

In the case of pH = 3, the specimen dipped in the pure 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution exhibited the lowest contact angle value, indicating that considerable hydrophilic corrosion products were formed on the surface of carbon steel, and the carbon steel was severely corroded. However, the contact angle increased from 45° under the blank system to 80° when the specimen was dipped in the aggressive medium containing N-CDs, implying that the N-CDs can improve the hydrophobicity of carbon steel and effectively isolate corrosive media. With the addition of the prepared capsule, the contact angle increased from 45° to 57°, which may be due to the insufficient release of N-CDs.

When the pH value was adjusted to 6, the contact angle remarkably increased to 79° with the increased release amount of N-CDs in the prepared capsule. However, the contact angle of the prepared capsule was still lower than that of the directly adding N-CDs (96°), indicating that the loaded N-CDs were still not entirely released at pH = 6. In the case of pH = 9, the contact angle of the prepared capsule was further improved, and the contact angle gap between the prepared capsule and the directly adding N-CDs was further reduced. This finding indicated that the prepared capsule could better improve the anticorrosion performance of the carbon steel due to the higher release amount in the alkaline environment. The results of the contact angle measurements demonstrated that the N-CDs could adsorb on the metal surface to form a hydrophobic film and improve the anticorrosion behavior of the sample. With the increase in pH value, the contact angle obtained from the prepared capsule gradually increased, further confirming that the release of N-CDs was favorable under the alkaline condition.

Fig. 10 illustrates the surface morphology of the specimen immersed in the aggressive solution for 3 h. As shown in Fig. 10(a–c), the optical image showed obvious corrosion traces on the surface of the sample. Obvious pitting pits were also observed in the microscopic SEM image, proving that the carbon steel was seriously corroded under the blank condition of pH = 3. With the introduction of the prepared capsules, the corrosion degree of the sample was not inhibited. The optical images showed that numerous yellow corrosion products were still observed on the surface of the sample, and obvious pitting pits were found in the SEM images. However, in the controlled experiment, the metal surface was still bright, and no pitting pits were found in the SEM image after the sample was directly immersed in the corrosive medium containing a certain concentration of N-CDS for 3 h, indicating that a sufficient amount of N-CDs can remarkably improve the anticorrosion performance of the sample. In the case of pH = 6, the specimen immersed in pure 3.5 wt. % NaCl was seriously corroded (Fig. 10(d)), and the corrosion traces were observed in the optical and SEM images. With the addition of the prepared capsule, the corrosion degree of the sample was greatly suppressed. Although the optical image showed that obvious gray-black corrosion products were still observed on the surface of the sample, no obvious pitting pits were found in the SEM image (Fig. 10(e)). This suppression in corrosion was due to the increased release of N-CD molecules in pH = 6. No obvious corrosion productions were observed when the pH value was increased to 9, as shown in Fig. 10(g–i). The optical and SEM images demonstrated the corrosion degree of the specimen immersed in the 3.5 wt.% NaCl containing the prepared capsule was remarkably reduced. Compared with the morphology images received from the specimen immersed in 3.5 wt.% NaCl containing with N-CDs, the optical and SEM images of the prepared capsule exhibited a similarly smooth surface to that of N-CDs, indicating the excellent corrosion resistance effect of the prepared capsule in alkaline conditions.
The results of the surface observation demonstrated that the prepared capsules could play an essential role in improving the anticorrosion performance of metal in high pH condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal route of CTAB in the preparation process of MSN
ce:section.ce:para: Two removal schemes are used for CTAB. The silicon oxide powder containing CTAB is directly calcined in a muffle furnace for a specific time to remove CTAB at high temperatures. The pore structure is formed in the silicon oxide during the CTAB gasification. Another method is to remove CTAB by refluxing the prepared silicon oxide in an ethanol solution containing concentrated hydrochloric acid for a specific time. In this study, we specifically discussed the effects of two schemes on MSN channels. Fig. 11 shows the TEM image of MSN obtained by calcination. Although MSN has a certain pore structure, the pore is extremely irregular, which indicates that the gasification rate of CTAB in different parts is inconsistent, and it is easy to form pores of different sizes in the calcination process. High-temperature calcination may cause the collapse of the MSN structure. On the contrary, the reaction conditions are mild, and the prepared silicon oxide has a regular pore structure by using a reflux scheme. Therefore, the reflux scheme is preferred to remove CTAB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of pH-responsive release for metal protection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 (a) illustrates the formation mechanism of the prepared capsule. The chemical structure of SA contains a large number of carboxylic acid groups and exhibits polyanionic behavior in solution. When calcium ions exist, sodium ions in SA can undergo ion exchange reaction with calcium ions, and the structure of SA is stacked to form a cross-linked network structure. Under acidic conditions, most carboxylic acid groups can combine with hydrogen ions to form carboxylic groups (--COOH), which weaken the electrostatic repulsion between anionic groups, thereby inhibiting the swelling of MSN@N-CDs@Alg and impede the release of N-CDs [52]. However, more carboxylic acid groups were ionized when the capsules were immersed in alkaline environments, which enhanced repulsion and promoted the expansion of the polymer matrix and the release of N-CDs. In addition, sodium alginate swells under alkaline conditions, resulting in a decrease in the physical strength of the entire capsule, and an increase in the internal space is favorable for the diffusion of N-CDs. In this way, the prepared capsule can control the release behavior of N-CDs.

When the corrosion process occurs on the metal surface, the cathode site of the metal begins to undergo an oxygen dissolution reaction, thereby forming hydroxide and increasing the pH value of the local site. COMSOL software was utilized to simulate the diffusion of hydroxide at the metallic interface and to further evaluate the changes in pH value in the cathode sites. We assumed that only oxygen consumption occurred at the cathode site. The reaction equation is expressed as follows:
(6)
O 2
+ 4
e −
+ 2
H 2
O → 4
O H
−

The results of polarization measurements demonstrated that the i corr value of carbon steel in 3.5 wt.% NaCl was 1.27 × 10−5 A/cm2, and the amount of hydroxide produced was 1.3 × 10−6 mol/(m2·s). The diffusion boundary layer thickness (δ) can be calculated with the following equation [53].
(7)
δ = − 5 ×
10
− 5
T 2
+ 3.015 ×
10
− 2
T − 4.144 ,
where T represents the temperature. In this research, the diffusion boundary layer thickness was 400 μm.

Based on the above conditions, we used software modeling to calculate the change in a concentration gradient of hydroxide from the metal surface to diffusion layer thickness. As shown in Fig. S3, the calculated c[OH−] on the metal surface was 9.7 × 10−4 mol/L, and the pH value in the cathode sites was 9.98. The theoretical simulation results confirmed that the alkaline environment generated at the cathode corrosion site could better satisfy the stimulation release of the prepared capsules.

In accordance with the above chemical structure analysis and theoretical calculation, the protection mechanism of the prepared capsule for metal is illustrated in Fig. 13 . Once the metal surface is corroded, the alkaline environment at the cathode will stimulate the release of N-CDs from the capsule, thereby improving the protection of the metal.

In this study, environmentally friendly N-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal reaction and were encapsulated into the interior of MSN by impregnation. Subsequently, a stimuli-release system was prepared via a simple cross-linking reaction of SA with calcium ions. The release kinetic of MSN@N-CDs@Alg confirmed that the prepared capsule obtained the property of pH stimuli-release, and the release rates were in the following order: alkaline > neutral > acid. Electrochemical tests and corrosion morphology characterization demonstrated that the fabricated capsules can remarkably improve the anticorrosion performance of metals in a high pH environment. In this study, we realized the intelligent release of N-CDs by combining the easily deactivated N-CDs with a controlled release system, which improved the service life of N-CDs. Furthermore, this stimuli-release system designed in this work provides a new strategy for metal protection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51871105) for their financial support and the Analysis Support of the Analytical and Testing Center, Huazhong University of Science and Technology.
